card.intuition: Precompute table[i][j] = min (or max, or GCD) of the range [i, i+2^j - 1]. For idempotent operations (min/max/GCD), any query [l, r] can be answered by two overlapping windows of size 2^k where k = floor(log2(r-l+1)) — overlapping doesn't double-count since min is idempotent.
card.analogy: Pre-measuring every possible 'power-of-two length' ruler on a number line. To find the minimum of any range, grab the two rulers of the right size that together cover the range — even if they overlap, taking the min of two mins is still the min.
card.time_complexity: O(n log n) build; O(1) query for idempotent operations; O(log n) query for non-idempotent (sum)
when_to_use: Static RMQ (no updates) — faster than segment tree for read-only ranges
LCA with Euler tour: reduce LCA to RMQ on the tour array
Any offline range aggregate on an immutable array
gotchas: Only supports STATIC arrays — no updates allowed (use segment tree for dynamic)
O(1) query only works for idempotent operations (min, max, GCD, AND, OR) — NOT for sum
Precompute log2 table to avoid floating-point log calls during queries

exercise.prompt: Implement a sparse table for O(1) range minimum queries.
hints: The right window starts at r - (1<<k) + 1 so that it exactly covers [r-(2^k)+1, r] — together with [l, l+2^k-1] these two windows cover [l, r]
exercise.test_code:
arr = [2,4,3,1,6,7,8,9,1,7]
st  = SparseTable(arr)
assert st.query(0,9) == 1
assert st.query(0,3) == 1
assert st.query(4,8) == 1
assert st.query(4,7) == 6
assert st.query(0,2) == 2
assert st.query(2,2) == 3   # single element

# Brute force verification
import random; random.seed(7)
for _ in range(200):
    l = random.randint(0, 9); r = random.randint(l, 9)
    assert st.query(l, r) == min(arr[l:r+1])
print('PASS')
